content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 15, 1976

John B. Allard
District Superintendent
Waterford Elementary School
Post Office Box 270
Waterford, California 95386

Dear Mr. Allard:

Senator Zenovich has provided our agency with a copy of your
letter of August 16, 1976, transmitting a resolution adopted by
the board of your school district. Essentially, the resolution
asks the Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974
to exempt elected school board members from the financial
disclosure provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et
seq.

It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable
to members and officials who are capable of affecting such
financial interests. Only those financial interests which
could be affected by the filer's official actions must be
disclosed, and it is the school district which has the first
and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts
of interest and provide for their disclosure. Hopefully,
individuals who object to overly broad disclosure will take an
active role in the design of each code's disclosure requirements,
so that intrusion of personal privacy will be kept at a minimum
consistent with the objective of preventing or disclosing
conflicts of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6461

I can see this image appears to be mostly blank or very faintly printed, with only some barely visible marks at the top that look like decorative elements or page artifacts. The rest of the page appears to be empty white space.

Since there is no clearly legible text content to transcribe from this document image, I cannot provide a meaningful transcription according to your rules.

John B. Allard
September 15, 1976
Page Two


The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

     Assets and income of public officials which
     may be materially affected by their official
     actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
     circumstances the officials should be dis-
     qualified from acting in order that conflicts
     of interest may be avoided.

                              Gov. Code § 81002(g).

Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

     Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I believe the proposal
is without merit. School boards make decisions of great con-
sequence to their communities. Like other public officials,
school board members serve in a position of public trust, and
the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by their own financial
interests. Disclosure of any financial interests which could
foreseeably be affected by their official actions is a reason-
able and effective means of preventing conflicts of interest.
In recent years the California Supreme Court and the courts of
many other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements
are valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of
the official is justified by the important public interest
that is served.

     I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-5444.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]
                              Michael Bennett
                              Executive Director

ATWATER SCHOOL DISTRICT
P.O. BOX 775
ATWATER, CALIFORNIA 95301

ASD RESOLUTION NO. 76-77-7

CONFLICT OF INTEREST

WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law
(Government Code Sections 87100 et. seq.) to prepare and
adopt a district Conflict of Interest Code, and

WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required
by law to file a disclosure statement disclosing their in-
terest and those of their immediate families in investments,
real property, and income, and

WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made avail-
able for public scrutiny, and

WHEREAS, board members of this district are unpaid officials,
and

WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is
an invasion of personal privacy of board members and their
immediate families; therefore,

BE IT RESOLVED by the Board of Trustees of the Atwater School
District to publicly state its opposition to the inclusion
of unpaid board members within the requirements of the Cali-
fornia "Political Reform Act of 1974" and to urge its legis-
lators to take necessary action to bring about the changes
needed to exclude said board members from these requirements.

The foregoing resolution was adopted at
a regular meeting of the Board of Trustees
of the Atwater School District on August 2,
1976.

Ayes: ___5___ Noes: ___0___ Absent: ___0___

SIGNED:_________________________
Thomas Olaeta, Secretary to the Board
of Trustees of the Atwater School District

REPLY TO:
□ STATE SENATOR GEORGE N. ZENOVICH
STATE CAPITOL
95814
DESK (916) 445-3600

SENATOR'S OFFICE
1115 "J" STREET, ROOM 22
POST OFFICE BOX 3289
95814
PHONE: (209) 521-3323

□

COMMITTEES
GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATION
INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS, CHAIRMAN
INSURANCE AND FINANCIAL
INSTITUTIONS
JUDICIARY

Senate
California Legislature

[seal image]

GEORGE N. ZENOVICH
FOURTEENTH SENATORIAL DISTRICT

TED C. HILLIARD
FIELD COORDINATOR

CHAIRMAN
SENATE COMMITTEE ON INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS

COMMITTEE ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814
PHONE: (916) 445-3221

August 25, 1976


John B. Allard
District Superintendent
Waterford Elementary School
P. O. Box 270
Waterford, California 95336

Dear Mr. Allard:

Thank you for your recent letter and the enclosed resolution
adopted by the Waterford School District Board of Education ex-
pressing the view that local school board members should be excluded
from the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.

The Act, at Section 87300, requires agencies to adopt and promul-
gate a conflict of interest code. "Agency" at Section 82003 includes
"local government agency". "Local government agency" includes school
district. Section 82041.

I sympathize with your view that these provisions are burdensome
on persons serving, in many cases, without any remuneration at all.
I assume, however, the drafters of the initiative (and perhaps the
public in voting for the initiative) perceived the real threat of
conflicts of interest in awarding contracts in the school district
as well as any other legislative or administrative body. In fact,
it is arguable, that since such a large percentage of a school board's
business is awarding contracts, such boards should be included.

In any event, since the Political Reform Act was an initiative
measure, it can only be amended by a vote of the people, except an
amendment "to further its purposes" could be passed by a two-thirds
vote of each House of the Legislature. A question arises as to
whether such an amendment would be construed to "further its purposes".
My staff is reviewing your suggestion and an appropriate remedy.

[illegible]

[illegible]
[illegible]

Mr. John B. Allard
August 25, 1976
Page 2


So the Commission may be aware of your views, I am forwarding
a copy of your letter to Mr. Robert Stern, General Counsel.

Sincerely,

[signature]
GEORGE N. ZENOVICH
State Senator

Waterford Elementary School
P. O. BOX 270                PHONE 874-2382
WATERFORD, CALIFORNIA 95386

John B. Allard
RICHARD M. MASON
DISTRICT SUPERINTENDENT
ALL PHONE MESSAGES

August 16, 1976


Honorable George N. Zenovich
14th Senate District
State Capitol Bldg., Room 2054
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Sir:

     The attached resolution was adopted by the
Waterford School District Board of Education at
the August 1976 regular meeting.

     It is their hope that you would make every
effort to see that elected members of school boards
serving the public schools of California be excluded
from the requirements of the Political Reform Act
of 1974.

     Thank you for your attention to this subject.

                                    Very sincerely,

                                    [signature]
                                    John B. Allard
                                    District Superintendent

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only a few faint marks visible at the top. The page is predominantly white/empty with minimal discernible text or content to transcribe.

RESOLUTION

WHEREAS, school board members are required by law to prepare
           and adopt a Conflict of Interest Code as required by
           the Political Reform Act of 1974, and

WHEREAS, each school board member is required by law to file a
           statement disclosing his interest and the interest of
           each member of his immediate family in income, real
           property, and investments, and

WHEREAS, each of these disclosure statements is made available
           to the public, and

WHEREAS, members of public school boards are already under
           controls which prevent them from being involved in
           activities which would be classified as conflicts of
           interest, and

WHEREAS, the California Political Control Act of 1974 is a
           further infringement of the rights and invasion of the
           privacy of these board members and their immediate
           families as private citizens, and

WHEREAS, this act will certainly require that each board member
           employ an accountant and an attorney to complete the
           necessary forms and to further guarantee his privacy
           and that of his immediate family, and

WHEREAS, each of these unpaid board members placed himself in
           a position which would encourage further investigation
           by individuals who have "an ax to grind," and

WHEREAS, the school districts of California need individuals
           with an understanding of finances, personnel relations,
           and successful business experiences to assist in the
           operation of our schools, and

WHEREAS, these resignations would place the public schools of
           California in an unnecessary position of peril,

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, That the Waterford School District
of Stanislaus County is unanimously opposed to the inclusion of
elected public school board members under the requirements of
the Political Reform Act of 1974, and requests action by our
duly elected legislators to take the necessary action to achieve
the exclusion of elected school board members from the requirements
of the California Political Reform Act of 1974.

                    Respectfully submitted,

                    [signature]
                    John B. Allard
                    Secretary to the Board,
                    Superintendent of the District
                    Waterford School District

Date: [signature] August 13, 1974

WEAVER UNION SCHOOL DISTRICT

ROY J. WARD, DISTRICT SUPERINTENDENT
3076 EAST CHILDS AVENUE
MERCED, CALIFORNIA 95340
(209) 723-2174

August 4, 1976

AUG 9 1976

State Senator George N. Zenovich
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Senator Zenovich:

Enclosed is a copy of a resolution passed by the
Weaver Board of Trustees at its regular meeting held
on August 2, 1976.

This resolution is in opposition to the inclusion of
elected public school board members within the require-
ments of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974."

It is our desire that legislators take the necessary
action to bring about the changes needed to exclude
school board members from the requirements of this act.

Your assistance in this matter will be appreciated

Very truly yours,

WEAVER UNION SCHOOL DISTRICT

Roy J. Ward
District Superintendent

I can see this image is almost entirely blank/white with only two small circular objects visible at the very top of the page. There is no readable text content to transcribe from this document image.

WASHINGTON UNION HIGH SCHOOL
Phone Fresno 485-8805
6041 So. Elm Ave. — Fresno, California 93706

RESOLUTION REGARDING CONFLICT OF INTEREST

WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law (Government
Code Section 87100 et seq.) to prepare and adopt a district
Conflict of Interest Code, and

WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required by law to
file a disclosure statement disclosing their interest and
those of their immediate families in investments, real property,
and income, and

WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made available for
public scrutiny, and

WHEREAS, other sections of the Government Code (Section 1090 et seq.)
and Title 2, California Administrative Code (Section 18700 et
seq.) and the Education Code (Section 1174) provide the needed
legal control of board member actions, and

WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is an infringement
on the rights of private citizens and their immediate families
and is an invasion of personal privacy of board members and
their immediate families, and

WHEREAS, this act can require personal expenses for attorney and accountant
fees by board members holding non-paying elective board positions,
and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of this Code will encourage
private investigative snooping by persons bent on personal vendettas,
and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of the California "Political Reform
Act of 1974" by boards will result in the resignation of untold
numbers of board members throughout the state,and

WHEREAS, school districts and communities of the State need citizens with
financial solvency, business acumen, and personal stability to
serve as board members.

IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED by the Board of Education of the Washington
Union High School District to publicly state its opposition to the
inclusion of elected public school board members within the requirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" and it is
resolved to urge its legislators to take the necessary action to
bring about the changes needed to exclude school board members from
the requirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974".

Accorded by Western Schools and Colleges Association
1970-1975

regular meeting of the Board which was open to the public
on July 22, 1976 as it appears in the minutes of the said
meeting of the said Governing Board.


                                    [signature]
                                    Frank S. Thompson
                                    District Supt./Secty of the Board

[Note: This document appears to be printed upside down. I will transcribe it as it appears, reading from the inverted orientation]

CONDITION TO OFFERING

WHEREAS, a certain instrument dated
March 28, 1960 between
NORMA KRAMER, Mortgagor

and per

WHEREAS, default has been made in the payment of the
installment of principal and interest due on said mortgage, and per

WHEREAS, the mortgagee desires to foreclose said mortgage
and to sell the mortgaged premises as provided by law; per

WHEREAS, notice of intention to foreclose said mortgage
has been duly given as required by law; per

WHEREAS, there has been no offer or bid made for said
mortgaged property at public sale; per

WHEREAS, the mortgagee desires to acquire said property
and to reinstate said mortgage; per

WHEREAS, all the holders of junior liens have been
notified of the proposed action; per

WHEREAS, the mortgagee is prepared to pay all costs
and charges incident to foreclosure proceedings; per

WHEREAS, it is desired to effect a settlement by and
between all parties in interest with respect to said mortgaged property;

NOW THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants
and agreements herein contained and other good and valuable consideration,
the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, it is
agreed as follows:

1. The mortgagee agrees to accept the property "as is" in
full settlement and satisfaction of all claims arising out of the mortgage
and note, provided the mortgagor will execute and deliver a deed to said
property in form satisfactory to mortgagee within thirty (30) days from
date hereof.

2. The mortgagor agrees to execute and deliver said deed
within the time specified above, and further agrees to vacate said
property and remove all personal property therefrom on or before the
date of execution of said deed.

3. All taxes, assessments and other charges against said
property shall be paid by mortgagor up to and including the date of
execution of said deed.

4. Mortgagor agrees to maintain fire and other necessary
insurance on said property until the date of execution of the deed.
